choices.text: They signal intent and urgency. Without prefixes, every comment can feel like equally-weighted criticism — authors don't know what's blocking vs preference. Prefixes give context: `nit:` (minor preference, not blocking — feel free to ignore), `blocking:` (must address before merge), `suggestion:` (proposed alternative; consider), `question:` (clarifying, not necessarily change), `praise:` (when something's good — builds positive culture). Authors prioritize blocking comments; reviewers don't accidentally hold up merges for trivia. Standard vocabulary (Conventional Comments) lets tools parse and report on review patterns. Reduces friction and miscommunication.

questions.explanation: Prefixes signal urgency (blocking vs nit), reduce friction, set clear expectations for action.

questions.prompt: Reviewer is comfortable with the code but has 5 minor style preferences (variable names, prefer const, formatting). Block the PR?

choices.text: No — leave nit-prefixed comments and Approve. 'Request changes' blocks merge entirely; reserve for substantive concerns (bugs, security issues, architectural problems). For style and naming preferences, comment with `nit:` and approve so the author can merge without waiting. Better: automate the boring stuff — formatters (Prettier, Black) handle formatting on save/commit; linters (ESLint, ruff) catch style violations; pre-commit hooks block commits with lint errors. When the machine catches it, humans don't have to review for it. Frees code review for substance — logic, design, tests, edge cases.

questions.explanation: Don't block PRs on style nits. Use formatter/linter automation and approve with nit comments.
